coredata.dc:title: A sensitive and selective triple-channel optical assay based on red-emissive carbon dots for the determination of PFOS
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Qian
Zhu, Panpan
Xiong, Jie
Gao, Lixia
Tan, Kejun
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a simple, fast and cost-effective triple-channel optical assay for the determination of PFOS has been developed. The carbon quantum dots (CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method and employed as probe for sensing PFOS. CDs react with PFOS to form ground-state complex, which brings about the intensity changes of three signals including fluorescence, UV–vis absorption and resonance light scattering (RLS). And the changes of the fluorescence and absorption signals can be used for the visual detection of PFOS. And there is a certain relationship between the three-signal changes with the concentration of PFOS, respectively. The proposed assay exhibits good selectivity and sensitivity with a limit of detection (LOD) of 18.27 nmol/L in the fluorescence signal. Fluorescence lifetime, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and zeta potential were investigated for the mechanism study. Furthermore, this promising approach was also successfully applied to PFOS sensing in real water samples with RSD ≤ 2.09%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS)
Carbon dots
Triple-channel
Visualization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of PFOS in water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21377103 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelength.

The changes of (A) fluorescence intensity, (C) absorption intensity, (D) RLS intensity at the different concentration of PFOS. Photograph of CDs in the presence of various concentrations of PFOS under (B) ultraviolet light (Ex = 365 nm) and (E) natural light.
Time-resolved decay of CDs without (black line) and with PFOS (red line).

TEM image of (A) CDs and (B) CDs-PFOS nanoparticles. (C) The particles size distribution of CDs. (D) Photograph of CDs in the presence or absence of PFOS in natural light. Concentration: CDs: 2 μg/L; PFOS: 10 μmol/L; pH, 5.4.
FT-IR spectra of PFOS (blue line), as-synthesized CDs (black line), CDs-PFOS system (red line).
Zeta potential of CDs and CDs-PFOS system in the optimum conditions. Concentration: CDs: 2 μg/L; PFOS: 10 μmol/L; pH, 5.4.

Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of PFOS or PFOA. Fluorescence quenching efficiency (F 0 − F) / F 0 in the presence of PFOS and PFOA was shown in the insets. Concentration: PFOS, 4.0 μmol/L; PFOA, 4.0 μmol/L; CDs, 0.5 μg/L.
Effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of PFOS. Concentration: PFOS, 4.0 μmol/L; CDs, 0.5 μg/L; pH, 5.4.
Effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of PFOS. Concentration: PFOS, 4.0 μmol/L; CDs, 0.5 μg/L; pH, 5.4.
Effect of reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the absence and presence of PFOS. Concentration: PFOS, 4.0 μmol/L; CDs, 0.5 μg/L; pH, 5.4.
The fluorescence intensity decreased value ∆F (F 0 − F) of CDs towards various perfluorinated compounds, respectively. PFCs, 4.0 μmol/L, CDs, 0.5 μg/L; pH, 5.4.
The calibration curve for the determination of PFOS characterized by (a) fluorophotometry, (b) spectrophotometry and (c) RLS spectra.
Schematic illustration of CDs-based triple-channel optical nanosensor for PFOS detection.
Tolerance concentration of potentially co-existing substances. a
The standard equations of the three signals and their related parameters.
Comparison with other sensors for the determination of PFOS.
Determination of PFOS in practical water samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The PFOS concentrations of all water samples are hardly detected by the present method. CDs, 0.5 μg/L; pH, 5.4.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Sample 1: Jialing River, the water samples were taking from the Jialing River in Beibei Dock.

head.ce:title: A sensitive and selective triple-channel optical assay based on red-emissive carbon dots for the determination of PFOS

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a simple, fast and cost-effective triple-channel optical assay for the determination of PFOS has been developed. The carbon quantum dots (CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method and employed as probe for sensing PFOS. CDs react with PFOS to form ground-state complex, which brings about the intensity changes of three signals including fluorescence, UV–vis absorption and resonance light scattering (RLS). And the changes of the fluorescence and absorption signals can be used for the visual detection of PFOS. And there is a certain relationship between the three-signal changes with the concentration of PFOS, respectively. The proposed assay exhibits good selectivity and sensitivity with a limit of detection (LOD) of 18.27 nmol/L in the fluorescence signal. Fluorescence lifetime, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and zeta potential were investigated for the mechanism study. Furthermore, this promising approach was also successfully applied to PFOS sensing in real water samples with RSD ≤ 2.09%.

ce:section.ce:para: Perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS, C8F17SO3 −), a typical perfluorooctane sulfonyl fluoride (POSF−) surfactant chemical, was an emerging contaminant added to the list of Stockholm Convention for global elimination in 2009 due to its detrimental effects on environment and human health [1–3]. Because of its prevalent and persistent nature, PFOS can notably accumulate in the environment [4,5]. N. Yamashita found that concentration of PFOS in open ocean water samples from the Pacific Ocean, Atlantic Ocean and several coastal seawater of Asian countries ranged from 1.2 g/L to 57,700 pg/L [6]. An important environment concern was that PFOS can bio-accumulate through food webs [7]. And it proved that the functional group of PFCs has effects on bio-accumulation. Thus PFOS with long-chain (number of carbon with fluorine bonds ≥ 8) and a sulfonic group was more likely to be retained than PFOA which is another most common PFCs [8]. Therefore, significant adverse human health effects of PFOS have been evaluated recently [9,10]. K. P. Lai firstly found that the hepatotoxic effects of prenatal exposure to PFOS may predispose to a long-term liver disorder in the offspring [11]. The U.S. Environment Protection Agency (EPA) addressed provisional short-term health advisory level for PFOS (200 ng/L) in drinking water, estimating a short-term safeguard public health level [12]. Given the world-wide interest in PFOS in the scientific community, the widespread environment accumulation and the potential health effects, it is time to say that detecting PFOS in water is of great importance.

For decades, conventional methods have been developed for analysis of PFOS, including high performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC/MS) analysis [13–15]. However, the two methods have their disadvantages such as pretreatment complexity, high cost and time consuming. Thus it still remains a challenge to develop a simple, fast and cost-effective method for the determination of PFOS. Recently, triple-channel optical assays have attracted significant attention as they integrate advantages of three different techniques consist of fluorescence, UV–vis and RLS, improving the reliability and accuracy of approach [16–18]. Consequently, it is essential to explore triple-channel optical assay for sensing of PFOS.

Carbon dots or carbon nanoparticles (CDs), owing to their superiority such as excellent luminescent properties, facile fabrication or functionalization and ease of synthesis, have attracted significant attention recently [19,20]. Compared with organic fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs are more friendly to environment with highly bio-compatibility, high water-solubility and photo-stability [21,22]. These properties make extensive application of CDs in sensing, photo-catalysis, bio-imaging and fluorescent ink etc. [23–25]. Herein, we report a CDs-based triple-channel optical assay for sensitive and selective determination of PFOS. CDs with red emission were synthesized by hydrothermal method using o‑phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid as raw materials. PFOS can react with CDs resulting in the changes of three signals comprising fluorescence quenching, decrease of absorption and enhancement of RLS. The naked-eye color and fluorescent response induced by changes of absorption and fluorescence can be used in visual monitor of PFOS. This assay can sense trace level of PFOS with a detection limit (LOD) of 18.27 nmol/L combining with fluorescent signal and exhibit selectivity as well as sensitivity in liquid phase. Moreover, the triple-channel optical assay has been successfully applied towards the sensing of PFOS in tap water and rivers with RSD ≤ 2.09%.

o‑Phenylenediamine(OPD) was commercially obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphoric acid was supplied by Tianjin Taixin Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS, 98.0%), perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA, 98.0%) and their homologues such as perfluoroheptanoic acid (PFHpA, 98.0%), heptafluorobutyric acid (PFBA, 98.0%), nonafluorovaleric acid (PFPeA, 98.0%), undecafluorohexanoic acid (PFHeA, 98.0%), perfluorononanoic acid (PFNA, 98.0%), perfluorodecanoic acid (PFDeA, 98.0%), pentafluoropropionic acid (PFPrA, 98.0%) were purchased from TCI Development Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments. Britton-Robinson buffer solution (BR) was used to control the acidity.

The fluorescence spectra and resonance Rayleigh scattering were recorded using a Hitachi F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The absorption spectra were recorded with a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The pH values were measured with a FE20/EL20 pH meter (Shanghai, China). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were collected using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Escalab250, Thermo, UK). Thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a thermal analyzer (STA449F3, Netzsch, Germany) under a flow of N2 gas at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on an X-ray diffractometer (Advance D8, Bruker, Germany) using Cu Kα radiation. The luminescence lifetime measurements were conducted on a FLS980 Series of Fluorescence Spectrometers (Edinburgh, U.K.). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were performed using a field emission transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV (JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd., Japan).Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured using a NEXUS-670 FT-IR spectrometer(NICOLET, USA). Zeta potentials were measured on zeta potential analyzers (Malvern, U.K.). Vortex mixer QL-901 (Haimen, China) was used for mixing.

CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment reported previously with some modification [26]. In a typical experiment, 0.4 g of OPD was mixed with 10 mL of phosphoric acid within a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and then heated at 200 °C for 10 h. When the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally, the navy blue product was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 20 min to remove large particles and the supernatants were collected. The supernatants were further purified in 24 h through a dialysis membrane tubing (500–1000 Da, molecular weight cut-off). The final solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further research.

Firstly 100 μL BR buffer (pH 5.4) and 50.0 μL CDs (10 μg/L) were mixed in a 2.0 mL centrifugation tube. Secondly, 200 μL of PFOS at different concentrations were introduced into the above prepared solution and the mixture was diluted to 1.0 mL with ultrapure water. Finally, the mixtures were incubated for 20 min at 20 °C. Controlled experiments were carried out by replacing PFOS with ultrapure water. The fluorescent intensity, absorption and RLS intensity of each sample was monitored.

As shown in Fig. 1 , fluorescence emission spectra of CDs were recorded under excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 600 nm. The fluorescence maximum is red-emissive at 620 nm independent of excitation wavelength. The emission intensity located at 450 nm is maximum when CDs are excited at 560 nm.

As shown in Fig. 2A, obvious fluorescence quenching of CDs are observed in the presence of PFOS ranging from 0 μmol/L to 12 μmol/L. The Fig. 2B displays the photograph of CDs in the presence of various concentrations of PFOS under ultraviolet light (Ex = 365 nm). It can be seen that the red fluorescence of CDs diminishes gradually, which indicates the probe has potential of fluorescent visualization for PFOS detection. The Fig. 2C shows the intensity of UV–Vis absorption at the maximum peak λ 618nm decreased with the increasing PFOS. Photograph in Fig. 2E exhibits the color of CDs in natural light changes from blue to colorless with the augment of PFOS. The RLS spectra were shown in Fig. 2D. It indicates that the intensities of RLS are enhanced by PFOS with the concentration of PFOS ranging from 0 μmol/L to 12 μmol/L. The above spectra demonstrate that the red-emissive CDs can be applied as triple-channel optical assay for the analysis of PFOS. Scheme 1 appears the schematic diagram of the synthesis of carbon dots and CDs-based triple-channel optical nanosensor for PFOS detection.

As well known, quenching mechanism of CDs can be divided into static quenching, dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), surface energy transfer (SET), Dexter energy transfer (DET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [27,28]. For static quenching, the formation of the ground-state complex can result in the change of the absorption spectrum of the CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2C, the absorption intensity of CDs declined gradually with the augment of PFOS, which was consistent with one of the characteristics of static quenching [29]. In order to further prove the static quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs and CDs-PFOS system were measured. As displayed in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was almost constant in the absence or presence of PFOS, indicating the ground-state complex formation between CDs and PFOS and the static quenching mechanism of the system.

To further verify the mechanism, CDs and CDs-PFOS were characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM), zeta potential measurements and FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 4A, the CDs were mono-dispersed and exhibited a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing value of 0.286 nm, which corresponds to the planes of graphitic carbon [30]. As shown in Fig. 4B, the diameters of CDs are distributed in the range of 1.2–3.2 nm with an average diameter of 2.2 ± 0.27 nm. Upon the addition of PFOS, larger particles with approximately dozens of nanometers were formed (Fig. 4C). And in Fig. 4D, the photograph demonstrates that there are precipitate in the presence of PFOS compared with pure CDs after the same time and speed of the centrifugation, which directly verifies the formation between PFOS and CDs. The surface functional groups of PFOS, CDs and CDs-PFOS were identified by FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 5 , the broad absorption bands at 1400–1000 cm−1 are attributed to the characteristic absorption of CF stretching vibration. And the broad absorption bands at 3150–3600 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The band at 1400 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption of CN stretching vibration. The broad absorption bands at 600–920 cm−1 are assigned to the blending vibration of NH [31,32]. It confirms that the existence of the amino groups on the surface of CDs, and the amino groups have positive charges under weakly acidic condition while PFOS molecules have negative charges. Thus there is an electrostatic interaction between them. Simultaneously, hydrogen bond can be formed between the oxygen atom of fluorine atom on the fluorocarbon bond of PFOS molecule and the hydrogen atom as well as nitrogen atom of the amino group on the surface of the CDs. Compared with the FTIR spectrum of CDs, the CDs-PFOS complex displays weakness of characteristic peaks of the CN, NH stretching vibrations and slight change of the NH blending vibration, indicating that the high affinity between PFOS and nitrogen-donor atoms from CDs. The combination between CDs and PFOS was further verified by the measurement of zeta potential. Figs. S4A and B show the change of CDs and CDs-PFOS system from single peak to double peaks, which indicate that parts of CDs have reaction with PFOS resulting in the decrease of Zeta potential. As shown in Fig. 6 , Zeta potential of CDs with +32.67 mV changes to CDs-PFOS system with +18.97 mV, indicating electrostatic attraction between the positively-charged CDs and negatively-charged PFOS. In conclusion, the aggregation between CDs and PFOS was successfully confirmed. Additionally, XPS, TGA and XRD of the synthesized CDs were examined. And the related results and discussions are shown in Supporting information (Figs. S1–S3).

To establish the sensitive detection of PFOS, a series of tests were performed to optimize the sensing parameters, such as acidity, electrolyte concentration, temperature, and reaction time. When the PFOS concentration was fixed at 4.0 μmol/L, the change of fluorescence of the CDs with or without PFOS at 620 nm upon addition of 0.5 μg/L CDs was used to optimize the sensing system.

When optimizing buffer varieties, four kinds of buffer were chosen including acetic acid‑sodium acetate buffer, phosphate buffer, KHP (potassium acid phthalate)-HCl buffer and BR buffer. As shown in Fig. S5, the best quenching efficiency was obtained in BR buffer medium. Considering stability and quenching efficiency of the system, BR was the best buffer and was chosen for further experiments.

To optimize the acidity, the impact of BR buffer in a wide pH range on the fluorescence quenching of CDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 , it was founded that acidity has effects on the reaction between CDs and PFOS. In order to find the best pH value, quenching efficiency (F 0 − F) / F 0 of the system was calculated. The inserted picture shows the quenching efficiency of the system reaches the maximum when pH is 5.4. From pH 2 to 5.4, the quenching efficiency increases gradually. It is possible that under the strong acidic conditions (pH < 5.4), the negative charge on the sulfonic acid group in PFOS is neutralized by H+, and the binding degree of CDs and PFOS is weakened. When pH is >5.4, the quenching efficiency reduces, which is because fewer positive charges on the surface of the carbon dots lessen the electrostatic interaction between PFOS and CDs [33]. Finally, pH 5.4 was selected as the optimal acidity for further experiments. At the same time, the effects of PFOA, another representative of perfluorinated compounds, on the quenching efficiency of CDs were also recorded under different pH in the range from 2 to 7. It was founded that the fluorescence signal almost has no responses to PFOA in BR buffer. The different phenomena between PFOS and PFOA might result from the higher hydrophobicity of PFOS than PFOA and higher electrostatic interaction between PFOS and CDs than PFOA [34]. Therefore, in the BR buffer solution of pH 5.4, PFOS can be selectively detected.

To optimize the ionic strength, NaCl was added to control the ionic strength of the system. Then a concentration of 0–0.8 mol/L was chosen to investigate the effect on quenching efficiency of CDs. As can be seen in the Fig. 8 , ionic strength strongly affects the fluorescence of the system. The quenching efficiency keeps lower than that of system without NaCl. It contributes that sodium chloride has a strong charge shielding effect on the system and interferes with the electrostatic reaction between NBA and PFOS. Hence, for reducing the influence of the ionic strength, NaCl was not added to the system.

In order to optimize the temperature of the system, different temperature (20 °C, 30 °C, 40 °C, 50 °C, 60 °C) was chosen. When the temperature is above 20 °C, the quenching efficiency of the system decreases with increasing temperature (Fig. 9 ). The high temperature results in aggravation of the molecular heat movement. Thus the weak electrostatic and the hydrophobic force between CDs and PFOS correspondingly lessen. In consideration of the stability of the reaction, 20 °C was selected as optimal temperature for all experiments.

The reaction time was optimized by comparing the fluorescent intensity of the CDs in the absence and in the presence of PFOS in 1–90 min after samples being mixed thoroughly under other optimum conditions. As shown in Fig. 10 , with the augment of reaction time, quenching efficiency increases in 30 min and keeps stable for the next hour. It indicates that reaction between CDs and PFOS completes about 30 min. All the experimental data were measured after 30 min.

In order to study the selectivity of the fluorescent CDs, several perfluorinated compounds, including PFOA, PFNA, PFHpA, PFHeA, PFBA, PFPeA, PFPrA, PFDeA were added into the reaction system replacing PFOS under optimum conditions. As shown in Fig. 11 , only PFOS causes the most obvious decrease of the fluorescent intensity of CDs, other compounds display no significant interference. It turns out that this fluorescent CDs have strong anti-interference ability and good specificity towards PFOS.

Possible interferences from metal ions, anion ions were checked. It was found that all the tested metal ions including Ca2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Fe3+ etc. at a concentration of 100 μmol/L exhibited no significant effect on the determination of PFOS with CDs (Table 1 ). The anion ions such as Ac−, SO4 2−, Cl−, EDTA, Br−, I−, CO3 2– shows weak effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs-PFOS. Additionally, it was founded that SDS and SDBS have strong influence on the system. Both tolerance concentrations of them are in the linear range and their effects can be eliminated by barium ion [35].

According to the above general procedure, the determination conditions were optimized as temperature = 20 °C, reaction time = 30 min, pH = 5.4. Under optimal conditions, the concentration-dependent manner for the PFOS-induced emission quenching of CDs was analyzed. As increasing the PFOS concentration, the fluorescence intensity of CDs became weak. As shown in Fig. 12A, the value of ∆F/F 0 (∆F = F 0 − F) was linearly dependent on the logarithm of the concentration of PFOS over the range of 0.5–12 μmol/L with a regression equation of ∆F/F 0 = 0.2221 ln (c + 0.0262) + 0.3755 (R 2 = 0.9999). The detection limit was estimated from the mean signal of the blank and three times the standard deviation of the blank. The LOD was calculated to be 18.27 nmol/L (3σ). Similarly, the absorption spectrum of CDs with a gradient concentration of PFOS was studied in Fig. 2B. It is found that the absorption intensity of CDs at λ 610nm decreases with an increase of the PFOS concentration. Fig. 12B exhibits the corresponding relationship between ∆A/A 0 (∆A = A 0 − A) versus micromole concentration ratio ranging from 0 to 8. Good linearity (R 2 = 0.9998) can be obtained with a LOD of 75.89 nmol/L. As depicted in Fig. 12C, the RLS spectrum of the CDs at various concentrations of PFOS gradually increases with the augment of PFOS concentration when λ is at 310 nm. This result reveals that the RLS intensity of the CDs is sensitive to PFOS concentration. The relationship between ∆I (∆I = I 0 − I) and the concentration of PFOS ranging from 0 to 12 μmol/L is represented in Fig. 12 C with the LOD of 120.45 nmol/L. The concrete parameters of the three signals are recorded in Table 2 . In addition, the results of comparison with some other methods for the assay of PFOS are listed in Table 3 . It can be seen that the detection limit of this method is comparable or lower than some other reported methods [34,36–39]. Compared with typical methods [40–44], this method is simple and low-cost without pre-concentration by using an inexpensive instrument. Therefore, it will be a valuable tool for the detection of PFOS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PFOS in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Several spiked water samples including tap water and Jialing River containing PFOS with three concentrations for PFOS detection were used to evaluate the application of present fluorescent assay. Prior to measurement, samples were heated to boiling and cooled naturally to room temperature firstly, and then samples were filtered three times by using a 0.22 μm filter membrane, the above operation repeated three times. Then after adding excessive Ba2+ to eliminate the interference of SDS and SDBS, cations exchange resin was used to eliminate interference of heavy metal cations and organic positive ion [45]. As displayed in Table 4 , the recoveries ranged from 97.9 to 104.8%, and the relative standard deviations ranged from 1.14 to 2.09%. The experimental results were satisfactory, so the reliability of this fluorescence assay was confirmed. In addition, the results indicate that the fluorescent assay provides a theoretical basis for application of PFOS determination in actual aqueous environment.

In summary, a triple-channel optical PFOS analytical system has been constructed based on the reaction between CDs and PFOS leading to three signals changes. Among them, fluorescence method with higher sensitivity enables the measurement of PFOS in the range of 0–12 μmol/L with a LOD of 18.27 nmol/L. Additionally the probe can be used as a visual sensor for PFOS detection in aqueous media. The proposed assay can be conducted in a simple, fast, reliable and cost-effective way and it also shows good selectivity. Moreover, this approach demonstrates satisfactory results for the determination of PFOS in water samples, indicating the prepared assay has promising application for the environmental monitoring and evaluating.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21377103).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2018.11.003.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Provisional Health Advisories for Perfluorooctanoic Acid (PFOA) and Perfluorooctane Sulfonate (PFOS)
